subsystem: Mutt/NeoMutt IMAP header-cache path builder. imap_hcache_open() takes an IMAP mailbox name (either an explicitly supplied path, or the mailbox parsed from the current IMAP context) and turns it into an on-disk header-cache file path, then opens that cache file via mutt_hcache_open(). It is the function that maps a server-side mailbox identity onto a local filesystem location.
provenance: Identified by the Mutt/NeoMutt-specific identifiers and calling conventions: the type header_cache_t*, the IMAP_DATA*/IMAP_MBOX/ciss_url_t types, the LONG_STRING size macro, the FREE() wrapper macro, and the helper functions imap_cachepath(), imap_parse_path(), mutt_account_tourl(), url_ciss_tostring(), and mutt_hcache_open() with the imap_hcache_namer callback and the global HeaderCache. The sample banner 'primevul_c_train_2666_fixed' and the eval id 'eval_0040_cwe22' indicate this is the post-fix revision of a CWE-22 (path traversal) issue; it was reviewed blind on its merits.
trust_boundary: The mailbox name is attacker-influenced. In the else-branch it is derived from idata->ctx->path parsed by imap_parse_path (line 14) into mx.mbox (line 17); in a hostile scenario a malicious or compromised IMAP server, or a MITM on an unencrypted connection, controls the mailbox names the client stores/opens. That name flows into mbox[] and ultimately into the filesystem path passed to mutt_hcache_open. The security question is whether a server-chosen mailbox name can steer the header-cache file to an attacker-chosen location outside the intended cache directory (path traversal). The four string guards at lines 21-25 are the entire sanitization layer.

what_the_code_does.code: header_cache_t* imap_hcache_open (IMAP_DATA* idata, const char* path)
what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point. It receives an IMAP session handle idata and an optional path string. It returns an opened header_cache_t* handle (or NULL on failure/refusal). The role of the function is to compute a stable local cache file name for a given IMAP mailbox and open that cache. Both parameters ultimately carry server-influenced data: path may be a caller-supplied mailbox, and when it is NULL the mailbox is taken from the live IMAP context whose contents the server drives.

Local state. mx holds the parsed IMAP mailbox structure (its .mbox member is a heap-allocated string produced by imap_parse_path). url is a ciss URL structure used to render the final cache path. cachepath and mbox are two fixed-size stack buffers sized by the LONG_STRING macro (a Mutt-wide constant, typically 1024). mbox holds the normalized mailbox name; cachepath holds the rendered URL/path string that becomes the on-disk filename. len is used to test the tail of mbox. Note that both buffers are fixed-size, so every routine that writes into them must respect the passed size argument.

If the caller supplied an explicit path, imap_cachepath is invoked to normalize it into mbox. imap_cachepath converts the IMAP mailbox name into a cache-friendly form (converting the server's hierarchy delimiter into '/', etc.) and is bounded by sizeof(mbox). The transformed result — not the raw input — is what the later guards inspect. mx is left unused/uninitialized on this branch (and correctly not freed).

Else-branch: with no explicit path, the mailbox is taken from the current IMAP context. It first guards that idata->ctx is non-NULL, then calls imap_parse_path on idata->ctx->path to populate mx (including allocating mx.mbox). If the context is absent or the path fails to parse, it returns NULL. This is the branch where the mailbox name is most directly server-influenced, because idata->ctx->path reflects the selected mailbox on the connection.

The parsed mailbox mx.mbox is normalized into mbox via imap_cachepath (again bounded by sizeof(mbox)), then the heap allocation mx.mbox is released with FREE (a Mutt macro that frees and NULLs the pointer). After this block, mbox contains the normalized mailbox string on both branches and the parse allocation is cleaned up.

First sanitization gate — the core of the CWE-22 fix. It rejects the mailbox if it contains an interior '/../' sequence (strstr), if it is exactly '..' (strcmp), or if it begins with '../' (strncmp of the first 3 bytes). These three checks are a denylist targeting the classic parent-directory traversal token '..' when delimited by '/'. If any matches, the function refuses to open a cache for that mailbox.

Second sanitization gate — the trailing-component case the first gate misses. It computes len = strlen(mbox) and, only when len > 3, compares the final three bytes against '/..', rejecting names that end in a parent-directory reference such as 'inbox/..'. Together with line 21 this is intended to cover leading, interior, exact, and trailing occurrences of a '..' path component.

The cache filename is rendered. mutt_account_tourl populates url from the connection's account (host, user, port, scheme) by dereferencing idata->conn->account. url.path is then pointed at the (validated) mbox string. url_ciss_tostring serializes the url into cachepath, bounded by sizeof(cachepath), with the U_PATH flag so the path component is included. cachepath is now the string that identifies this mailbox's cache file.

Finally the header cache is opened. mutt_hcache_open combines the configured HeaderCache base directory, the cachepath identity string, and the imap_hcache_namer callback (which hashes/derives the concrete on-disk filename) to open (creating if needed) the cache file. This is the sink: whatever survived the guards above becomes part of a real filesystem path that is created and written.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Central contract (CWE-22). The mailbox name is server-influenced, and it becomes part of a filesystem path opened for writing at line 31. The only defense is this hand-rolled denylist of literal '..' patterns. Denylisting is inherently fragile compared to canonicalizing the resolved path and confirming it stays under the HeaderCache directory: the guard must anticipate every representation of a parent-directory component, and any missed form yields a traversal that writes/reads a header-cache file outside the cache directory (potential file overwrite/corruption, or cache poisoning). The safe invariant is 'the final resolved absolute path is a prefix-descendant of the header-cache root', which a string denylist cannot guarantee.

Boundary off-by-one in the trailing guard. The predicate requires len > 3, so a mailbox that normalizes to exactly the three-byte string '/..' (len == 3) is NOT caught here. Check it against the other gates: strstr(mbox, "/../") does not match (no trailing slash), strcmp(mbox, "..") does not match, and strncmp(mbox, "../", 3) compares '/..' vs '../' and does not match. So the exact value '/..' — a bare parent-directory reference — slips through all four checks. The guard should use len >= 3 (or compare the tail whenever len >= 3) so the len == 3 case is included. Whether '/..' is independently reachable depends on what imap_cachepath can emit, but the guard as written has a real gap for the shortest traversal token, which is exactly the kind of edge a hostile mailbox name probes.

Separator- and encoding-specific denylist. Every check hard-codes '/' as the path separator and the literal ASCII bytes '..'. It does not consider: backslash separators ('..\\..\\', relevant if the cache path is ever consumed on a platform or by a library that treats '\\' as a separator), a lone interior '..' component that is not framed by '/' on both sides in the way strstr('/../') expects (e.g. patterns produced by delimiter substitution in imap_cachepath), NUL-embedded or percent-encoded forms, or an absolute-path style leading component. Because the mailbox delimiter is server-chosen and imap_cachepath rewrites it, the exact byte layout reaching this test is not fully under the caller's control, so a denylist keyed to one separator is easy to sidestep. Same CWE-22 invariant as above: prefer canonicalize-then-contain over pattern matching.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
url.path = mbox;
  url_ciss_tostring (&url, cachepath, sizeof (cachepath), U_PATH);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Validate-one-representation, use-another. The four guards inspect mbox, but the value that actually forms the filesystem path is cachepath, produced by url_ciss_tostring after url.path is set to mbox. If url_ciss_tostring performs any transformation on the path component under U_PATH (URL-style encoding/decoding, delimiter handling, or truncation to sizeof(cachepath)), the string that reaches mutt_hcache_open at line 31 is not the exact string that was validated. Truncation in particular can drop a trailing sanitized suffix or change component boundaries. The invariant to confirm is that url_ciss_tostring cannot re-introduce a traversal token (or alter path semantics) relative to the already-validated mbox, otherwise the check is being applied to the wrong artifact.

Fixed-buffer NUL-termination / truncation contract (latent, CWE-125/CWE-787 in the callee). mbox and cachepath are LONG_STRING stack buffers. The safety of the subsequent strstr/strlen/strcmp at lines 21-25 depends on imap_cachepath always NUL-terminating mbox within sizeof(mbox); if it can leave mbox unterminated on truncation, the string scans read past the buffer (out-of-bounds read) and the guards operate on garbage. Likewise url_ciss_tostring must respect sizeof(cachepath). Also note a subtler risk: if imap_cachepath silently truncates a long mailbox name, the truncation could remove a trailing token in a way that changes whether a traversal pattern is present. Confirm both helpers bound-and-terminate and that truncation cannot defeat the guards.

Unchecked handle dereference (CWE-476, low/latent). Unlike line 14, which guards idata->ctx, this line dereferences idata->conn (idata->conn->account) with no NULL check, as does idata itself throughout. If imap_hcache_open is ever reachable with a session whose conn is not yet established (error/teardown paths), this is a NULL-pointer dereference and crash. It is not attacker-controlled memory corruption, but it is an unvalidated invariant: the implementation must guarantee idata->conn is non-NULL at every call site.

summary: This is the hardened revision of a Mutt/NeoMutt IMAP header-cache path-traversal (CWE-22): a server-influenced mailbox name is normalized into a fixed buffer and used to build the on-disk cache filename, with four string guards (lines 21-25) as the sole defense. The mitigation is a literal-pattern denylist rather than a canonicalize-and-contain check, and it has a concrete boundary gap — the len > 3 test at line 24 fails to reject a mailbox that reduces to exactly '/..'. Residual risk is medium: the primary traversal vectors are blocked, but the denylist is separator/encoding-specific, is applied to mbox while the on-disk path is derived from cachepath, and depends on unverified NUL-termination and non-NULL handle contracts.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: imap_hcache_open(IMAP_DATA* idata, const char* path) -> header_cache_t* (line 2)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Maps a server-influenced mailbox name onto a filesystem path; the whole path-traversal surface lives here.
Local parse result; mx.mbox is freed on line 18 and unused on the path branch — no lifetime/leak issue in the shown code.
Local URL builder; carries no attacker length/buffer field itself, only aggregates account + validated path.
Fixed-size sink buffer that becomes the on-disk filename; depends on url_ciss_tostring honoring sizeof and terminating.
Fixed-size buffer holding the normalized, server-influenced mailbox name; NUL-termination is a precondition for the guards' string scans.
Drives the trailing-'/..' boundary test at line 24 whose len > 3 condition mis-handles the len == 3 case.
Fixed capacity constant; risk is in whether callees respect it, tracked under the buffer anchors.
Normalizes untrusted mailbox into the fixed buffer; must bound and NUL-terminate and must not truncate in a way that defeats the guards.
Correctly NULL-checks idata->ctx before deref and handles parse failure by returning NULL.
Frees the heap mailbox on the branch that allocated it; NULLs the pointer, no double-free or leak visible.
Separator- and encoding-specific denylist; the primary but incomplete CWE-22 mitigation.
Boundary off-by-one: len > 3 excludes the exact '/..' (len == 3) case.
Dereferences idata->conn without a NULL check (unlike idata->ctx on line 14).
Binds the validated string into the URL; validation target (mbox) differs from the emitted sink (cachepath).
Transforms/serializes path into the sink buffer; could re-encode or truncate the already-validated value.
The sink call itself; the traversal risk is fully captured by the cachepath/guard anchors feeding it.

findings.title: IMAP-server-influenced mailbox name reaches header-cache filesystem path, defended only by a literal '..' denylist
findings.bug_class: Path traversal via denylist instead of canonicalize-and-contain
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0040_cwe22.c (guards here; sink is mutt_hcache_open in implementation, file not provided)
findings.invariant: The final resolved on-disk cache path must be a descendant of the configured HeaderCache directory for every possible server-supplied mailbox name; enforce by canonicalizing the resolved path and rejecting anything that escapes the root, not by string-matching '..' tokens.
findings.failure_mode: A malicious/compromised IMAP server (or MITM on a plaintext connection) supplies a mailbox name that normalizes past this denylist; the surviving name is rendered into cachepath and passed to mutt_hcache_open at line 31, causing a header-cache file to be created/written outside the cache directory (file overwrite/corruption or cache poisoning). The denylist covers the common leading/interior/exact/trailing '..' forms but cannot prove containment.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether every reachable output of imap_cachepath that escapes the cache root is rejected here, and whether mutt_hcache_open/imap_hcache_namer re-anchor the path under HeaderCache regardless of cachepath content.

findings.title: Off-by-one in trailing-'/..' guard lets the exact string '/..' pass all four checks
findings.bug_class: Incomplete input validation (boundary off-by-one in denylist)
findings.invariant: The trailing parent-directory check must fire for any mbox whose length is at least 3 and whose last three bytes are '/..'; the length precondition must be len >= 3, not len > 3.
findings.failure_mode: When mbox is exactly '/..' (len == 3), line 24's len > 3 condition is false so the tail is never compared; and line 21's checks also miss it (strstr wants '/../', strcmp wants '..', strncmp compares '/..' vs '../'). The bare parent-directory reference '/..' therefore slips through the entire sanitizer. Reachability depends on what imap_cachepath can emit, but the guard is demonstrably incomplete for the shortest traversal token.
findings.what_to_confirm: That imap_cachepath cannot produce a normalized mbox equal to '/..' (or ending in '/..' at len == 3), and change the predicate to len >= 3 to close the gap.

findings.title: Denylist is separator- and encoding-specific ('/' and literal ASCII '..' only)
findings.bug_class: Denylist bypass (alternate separators / encodings not covered)
findings.invariant: Traversal rejection must be independent of separator character and byte encoding; any component that resolves to a parent directory must be rejected regardless of how it is spelled.
findings.failure_mode: The checks hard-code '/' and literal '..'. Backslash-based sequences, delimiter forms introduced by imap_cachepath's server-delimiter substitution, or any encoded/alternate representation of a '..' component are not detected. If any such form is honored by the downstream path resolution (url_ciss_tostring, mutt_hcache_open, or the OS), traversal is possible despite the guard.
findings.what_to_confirm: Which separator(s) imap_cachepath emits and whether url_ciss_tostring / mutt_hcache_open treat any character other than '/' as a path separator; verify the guard covers every such separator.

findings.title: Validation applied to mbox but on-disk path is derived from cachepath after url_ciss_tostring
findings.anchor: url.path = mbox; url_ciss_tostring (&url, cachepath, sizeof (cachepath), U_PATH)
findings.code:
url.path = mbox;
  url_ciss_tostring (&url, cachepath, sizeof (cachepath), U_PATH);
findings.invariant: The string that reaches mutt_hcache_open must be path-equivalent to the string the guards validated; url_ciss_tostring must not re-introduce traversal semantics, decode encoded tokens, or truncate the path in a way that changes component boundaries.
findings.failure_mode: The guards inspect mbox, but line 29 serializes url (with url.path = mbox) into cachepath under U_PATH, and line 31 uses cachepath, not mbox. If url_ciss_tostring performs URL-style encode/decode or truncates to sizeof(cachepath), the emitted path can differ from what was validated, defeating the check.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether url_ciss_tostring with U_PATH alters the path component (encoding/decoding) or can truncate, and whether the result can differ in traversal-relevant ways from the validated mbox.

findings.title: idata->conn dereferenced without NULL check
findings.invariant: idata and idata->conn must be non-NULL at every call to imap_hcache_open; if that cannot be guaranteed on error/teardown paths, the access must be guarded like idata->ctx is on line 14.
findings.failure_mode: Line 14 defensively checks idata->ctx, but line 27 dereferences idata->conn->account with no such check. If the function is reachable with a session whose connection is not established (or being torn down), this is a NULL dereference and crash (denial of service). Not memory-corrupting, but an unvalidated invariant.
findings.what_to_confirm: That all callers hold a fully-connected idata with a non-NULL conn before invoking imap_hcache_open.

findings.title: Guards and sink rely on unverified NUL-termination/bounding of the fixed LONG_STRING buffers
findings.anchor: imap_cachepath(..., mbox, sizeof(mbox)); char mbox[LONG_STRING]; char cachepath[LONG_STRING]
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read on unterminated buffer / truncation defeating validation
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0040_cwe22.c (imap_cachepath / url_ciss_tostring implementations, files not provided)
findings.invariant: imap_cachepath must always NUL-terminate mbox within sizeof(mbox), and url_ciss_tostring must always NUL-terminate cachepath within sizeof(cachepath); truncation of an overlong mailbox must not silently drop a token that changes traversal detection.
findings.failure_mode: The strstr/strlen/strcmp scans at lines 21-25 assume mbox is NUL-terminated; if imap_cachepath can leave it unterminated on truncation, those scans read past the buffer (OOB read). Separately, if a long server mailbox is truncated, the truncation could alter whether a '..' component is present at the tail, weakening the guards.
findings.what_to_confirm: That imap_cachepath and url_ciss_tostring guarantee NUL-termination within the given size and that truncation cannot change the traversal-relevant suffix of mbox.

audit_checklist: Confirm mutt_hcache_open + imap_hcache_namer re-anchor cachepath under the HeaderCache root (e.g. via hashing) so a traversal component in cachepath cannot escape the cache directory.
Change line 24's condition from len > 3 to len >= 3 (or otherwise handle the exact '/..' case) and verify imap_cachepath cannot emit '/..' or a len==3 traversal token.
Determine every path-separator imap_cachepath and url_ciss_tostring recognize; ensure the denylist covers each (not just '/'), or replace the denylist with canonicalize-then-contain.
Verify url_ciss_tostring under U_PATH does not decode/encode or truncate the path in a way that reintroduces or hides '..' relative to the validated mbox.
Confirm imap_cachepath and url_ciss_tostring always NUL-terminate within the provided sizeof() bounds so the line 21-25 string scans cannot read out of bounds.
Guard idata->conn (line 27) against NULL, consistent with the idata->ctx check on line 14, or prove it is always non-NULL at call sites.
Check that mx.mbox is always freed exactly once (line 18) on the parse branch and never used after FREE.
